mn60:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā*, wandering on tour among the Kosalans together with a large *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghena*], arrived at Sālā, a brahmin village of the Kosalans.
mn60:10.1: Now in this case, householders, those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:10.2: ‘There is gift … who having directly known and realized for themselves this world and the other world make them known’—
mn60:10.3: it can be expected of them that, having turned away from these three unwholesome dhammas—bad bodily conduct, bad verbal conduct, and bad mental conduct—they will undertake and practice these three wholesome dhammas—good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct.
mn60:10.5: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do see the danger, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome dhammas, and the benefit in renunciation and the side of purification in wholesome dhammas.
mn60:11.1: Though the other world truly exists, he holds the view, ‘There is another world’; that is his right view.
mn60:11.2: Though the other world truly exists, he intends, ‘There is another world’; that is his right thought.
mn60:11.3: Though the other world truly exists, he speaks the words, ‘There is another world’; that is his right speech.
mn60:11.4: Though the other world truly exists, he says, ‘There is another world’; and he does not make himself opposed to those *arahants* who know the other world.
mn60:11.5: Though the other world truly exists, he persuades another, ‘There is another world’; that is persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*].
mn60:11.6: And through that persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*], he neither raises himself up nor puts others down.
mn60:11.7: Thus his bad conduct has already been abandoned, and good conduct is present—
mn60:11.8: and there are also this right view, right thought, right speech, non-opposition to the noble ones, persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*], no self-exaltation, and no disparagement of others. In this way these many wholesome dhammas come to be for him because of right view.
mn60:12.1: In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus:
mn60:12.2: ‘If there is another world, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will be reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn60:12.3: And even setting aside the question of the other world, and granting that what those ascetics and brahmins say is true,
mn60:12.4: still this person is praised by the wise here and now: “This person is virtuous, of right view, and one who affirms existence.”
mn60:12.5: But if there really is another world, then this person wins both ways—
mn60:12.6: both because he is praised by the wise here and now, and because on the breakup of the body, after death, he will be reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn60:12.7: So this sure principle, well grasped and adopted by him, covers both sides and leaves out the unwholesome alternative.
mn60:13.1: There are, householders, some ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:13.2: ‘For one who acts and causes others to act, who cuts and causes others to cut, who cooks and causes others to cook, who grieves and causes others to grieve, who exhausts himself and causes others to be exhausted, who trembles and causes others to tremble, who kills living beings, takes what is not given, breaks into houses, plunders, commits burglary, ambushes travellers on the road, goes to another’s wife, and speaks falsely—for one acting thus, no evil is done.
mn60:13.3: Even if with a razor-edged wheel someone were to make the living beings on this earth into one mass of flesh, one heap of flesh, from that there would be no evil, no coming of evil.
mn60:13.4: Even if he were to go along the southern bank of the Ganges killing, slaughtering, cutting, causing others to cut, cooking, causing others to cook, from that there would be no evil, no coming of evil.
mn60:13.5: Even if he were to go along the northern bank of the Ganges giving, causing others to give, sacrificing, causing others to sacrifice, from that there would be no merit, no coming of merit.
mn60:13.6: Through giving, self-control, restraint, and truthful speech there is no merit, no coming of merit.’
mn60:14.1: And among those very ascetics and brahmins, householders, there are some ascetics and brahmins who are direct opponents. They say this:
mn60:14.2: ‘For one who acts and causes others to act, who cuts and causes others to cut, who cooks and causes others to cook, who grieves and causes others to grieve, who exhausts himself and causes others to be exhausted, who trembles and causes others to tremble, who kills living beings, takes what is not given, breaks into houses, plunders, commits burglary, ambushes travellers on the road, goes to another’s wife, and speaks falsely—for one acting thus, evil is done.
mn60:14.3: Even if with a razor-edged wheel someone were to make the living beings on this earth into one mass of flesh, one heap of flesh, from that there would be evil, there would be a coming of evil.
mn60:14.4: Even if he were to go along the southern bank of the Ganges killing, slaughtering, cutting, causing others to cut, cooking, causing others to cook, from that there would be evil, there would be a coming of evil.
mn60:14.5: Even if he were to go along the northern bank of the Ganges giving, causing others to give, sacrificing, causing others to sacrifice, from that there would be merit, there would be a coming of merit.
mn60:14.6: Through giving, self-control, restraint, and truthful speech there is merit, there is a coming of merit.’
mn60:14.8: are not those ascetics and brahmins direct opponents of one another?”
mn60:15.1: “Now in this case, householders, those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:15.2: ‘For one who acts and causes others to act, who cuts and causes others to cut, who cooks and causes others to cook, who grieves and causes others to grieve, who exhausts himself and causes others to be exhausted, who trembles and causes others to tremble, who kills living beings, takes what is not given, breaks into houses, plunders, commits burglary, ambushes travellers on the road, goes to another’s wife, and speaks falsely—for one acting thus, no evil is done.
mn60:15.3: Even if with a razor-edged wheel someone were to make the living beings on this earth into one mass of flesh, one heap of flesh, from that there would be no evil, no coming of evil.
mn60:15.4: Even if he were to go along the southern bank of the Ganges killing, slaughtering … through giving, self-control, restraint, and truthful speech there is no merit, no coming of merit’—
mn60:15.5: it can be expected of them that, having turned away from these three wholesome dhammas—good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct—they will undertake and practice these three unwholesome dhammas—bad bodily conduct, bad verbal conduct, and bad mental conduct.
mn60:15.7: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do not see the danger, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome dhammas, nor the benefit in renunciation and the side of purification in wholesome dhammas.
mn60:16.1: Though action truly exists, he holds the view, ‘There is no action’; that is his wrong view.
mn60:16.2: Though action truly exists, he intends, ‘There is no action’; that is his wrong thought.
mn60:16.3: Though action truly exists, he speaks the words, ‘There is no action’; that is his wrong speech.
mn60:16.4: Though action truly exists, he says, ‘There is no action’; and he makes himself opposed to those *arahants* who affirm action.
mn60:16.5: Though action truly exists, he persuades another, ‘There is no action’; that is persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*].
mn60:16.6: And through that persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*], he raises himself up and puts others down.
mn60:16.7: Thus his good conduct has already been abandoned, and bad conduct is present—
mn60:16.8: and there are also this wrong view, wrong thought, wrong speech, opposition to the noble ones, persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*], self-exaltation, and disparagement of others. In this way these many evil, unwholesome dhammas come to be for him because of wrong view.
mn60:17.1: In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus:
mn60:17.2: ‘If there is no action, then on the breakup of the body this person will have made himself safe.
mn60:17.3: But if there is action, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn60:17.4: And even setting aside the question of action, and granting that what those ascetics and brahmins say is true,
mn60:17.5: still this person is criticized by the wise here and now: “This person is immoral, of wrong view, and one who denies action.”
mn60:17.6: But if there really is action, then this person loses both ways—
mn60:17.7: both because he is criticized by the wise here and now, and because on the breakup of the body, after death, he will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn60:17.8: So this sure principle, badly grasped and adopted by him, covers only one side and misses the wholesome alternative.
mn60:18.1: Now in this case, householders, those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:18.2: ‘For one who acts and causes others to act, who cuts and causes others to cut, who cooks and causes others to cook, who grieves and causes others to grieve, who exhausts himself and causes others to be exhausted, who trembles and causes others to tremble, who kills living beings, takes what is not given, breaks into houses, plunders, commits burglary, ambushes travellers on the road, goes to another’s wife, and speaks falsely—for one acting thus, evil is done.
mn60:18.3: Even if with a razor-edged wheel someone were to make the living beings on this earth into one mass of flesh, one heap of flesh, from that there would be evil, there would be a coming of evil.
mn60:18.4: Even if he were to go along the southern bank of the Ganges killing, slaughtering, cutting, causing others to cut, cooking, causing others to cook, from that there would be evil, there would be a coming of evil.
mn60:18.5: Even if he were to go along the northern bank of the Ganges giving, causing others to give, sacrificing, causing others to sacrifice, from that there would be merit, there would be a coming of merit. Through giving, self-control, restraint, and truthful speech there is merit, there is a coming of merit’—
mn60:18.6: it can be expected of them that, having turned away from these three unwholesome dhammas—bad bodily conduct, bad verbal conduct, and bad mental conduct—they will undertake and practice these three wholesome dhammas—good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct.
mn60:18.8: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do see the danger, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome dhammas, and the benefit in renunciation and the side of purification in wholesome dhammas.
mn60:19.1: Though action truly exists, he holds the view, ‘There is action’; that is his right view.
mn60:19.2: Though action truly exists, he intends, ‘There is action’; that is his right thought.
mn60:19.3: Though action truly exists, he speaks the words, ‘There is action’; that is his right speech.
mn60:19.4: Though action truly exists, he says, ‘There is action’; and he does not make himself opposed to those *arahants* who affirm action.
mn60:19.5: Though action truly exists, he persuades another, ‘There is action’; that is persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*].
mn60:19.6: And through that persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*], he neither raises himself up nor puts others down.
mn60:19.7: Thus his bad conduct has already been abandoned, and good conduct is present—
mn60:19.8: and there are also this right view, right thought, right speech, non-opposition to the noble ones, persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*], no self-exaltation, and no disparagement of others. In this way these many wholesome dhammas come to be for him because of right view.
mn60:2.1: The brahmin householders of Sālā heard:
mn60:2.2: “Indeed, Master Gotama, the ascetic, a Sakyan son gone forth from the Sakyan clan, wandering on tour among the Kosalans together with a large *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghena*], has arrived at Sālā.
mn60:2.3: Now concerning that Master Gotama, this fine report has arisen:
mn60:2.4: ‘The *Bhagavā* is an *arahant*, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, fortunate, knower of the world, unsurpassed guide of persons to be tamed, teacher of devas and humans, awakened, the *Bhagavā*.’
mn60:2.5: Having directly known and realized for himself this world with its devas, its Māras, its Brahmās, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans, he makes it known.
mn60:2.6: He teaches the *dhamma* that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, with meaning and wording; he reveals a holy life that is complete, wholly full, and pure.
mn60:2.7: It is good indeed to see such *arahants*.”
mn60:20.1: In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus:
mn60:20.2: ‘If there is action, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will be reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn60:20.3: And even setting aside the question of action, and granting that what those ascetics and brahmins say is true,
mn60:20.4: still this person is praised by the wise here and now: “This person is virtuous, of right view, and one who affirms action.”
mn60:20.5: But if there really is action, then this person wins both ways—
mn60:20.6: both because he is praised by the wise here and now, and because on the breakup of the body, after death, he will be reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn60:20.7: So this sure principle, well grasped and adopted by him, covers both sides and leaves out the unwholesome alternative.
mn60:21.1: There are, householders, some ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:21.2: ‘There is no cause, there is no condition for the defilement of beings;
mn60:21.3: beings are defiled without cause and without condition.
mn60:21.4: There is no cause, there is no condition for the purification of beings;
mn60:21.5: beings are purified without cause and without condition.
mn60:21.6: There is no strength, no energy, no human endurance, no human effort;
mn60:21.7: all beings, all living creatures, all creatures, all lives are powerless, weak, without energy, shaped by destiny, circumstance, and nature, and experience *dukkha* and *sukha* in exactly six classes of birth.’
mn60:22.1: And among those very ascetics and brahmins, householders, there are some ascetics and brahmins who are direct opponents.
mn60:22.10: are not those ascetics and brahmins direct opponents of one another?”
mn60:22.3: ‘There is cause, there is condition for the defilement of beings;
mn60:22.4: beings are defiled through cause and condition.
mn60:22.5: There is cause, there is condition for the purification of beings;
mn60:22.6: beings are purified through cause and condition.
mn60:22.7: There is strength, there is energy, there is human endurance, there is human effort;
mn60:22.8: not all beings, all living creatures, all creatures, all lives are powerless, weak, without energy, shaped by destiny, circumstance, and nature, and experiencing *dukkha* and *sukha* in exactly six classes of birth.’
mn60:23.1: “Now in this case, householders, those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:23.2: ‘There is no cause, there is no condition for the defilement of beings;
mn60:23.3: beings are defiled without cause and without condition.
mn60:23.4: There is no cause, there is no condition for the purification of beings;
mn60:23.5: beings are purified without cause and without condition.
mn60:23.6: There is no strength, no energy, no human endurance, no human effort; all beings, all living creatures, all creatures, all lives are powerless, weak, without energy, shaped by destiny, circumstance, and nature, and experience *dukkha* and *sukha* in exactly six classes of birth’—
mn60:23.7: it can be expected of them that, having turned away from these three wholesome dhammas—good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct—they will undertake and practice these three unwholesome dhammas—bad bodily conduct, bad verbal conduct, and bad mental conduct.
mn60:23.9: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do not see the danger, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome dhammas, nor the benefit in renunciation and the side of purification in wholesome dhammas.
mn60:24.1: Though cause truly exists, he holds the view, ‘There is no cause’; that is his wrong view.
mn60:24.2: Though cause truly exists, he intends, ‘There is no cause’; that is his wrong thought.
mn60:24.3: Though cause truly exists, he speaks the words, ‘There is no cause’; that is his wrong speech.
mn60:24.4: Though cause truly exists, he says, ‘There is no cause’; and he makes himself opposed to those *arahants* who affirm cause.
mn60:24.5: Though cause truly exists, he persuades another, ‘There is no cause’; that is persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*].
mn60:24.6: And through that persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*], he raises himself up and puts others down.
mn60:24.7: Thus his good conduct has already been abandoned, and bad conduct is present—
mn60:24.8: and there are also this wrong view, wrong thought, wrong speech, opposition to the noble ones, persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*], self-exaltation, and disparagement of others. In this way these many evil, unwholesome dhammas come to be for him because of wrong view.
mn60:25.1: In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus:
mn60:25.2: ‘If there is no cause, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will have made himself safe.
mn60:25.3: But if there is cause, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn60:25.4: And even setting aside the question of cause, and granting that what those ascetics and brahmins say is true,
mn60:25.5: still this person is criticized by the wise here and now: “This person is immoral, of wrong view, and one who denies cause.”
mn60:25.6: But if there really is cause, then this person loses both ways—
mn60:25.7: both because he is criticized by the wise here and now, and because on the breakup of the body, after death, he will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn60:25.8: So this sure principle, badly grasped and adopted by him, covers only one side and misses the wholesome alternative.
mn60:26.1: Now in this case, householders, those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:26.2: ‘There is cause, there is condition for the defilement of beings;
mn60:26.3: beings are defiled through cause and condition.
mn60:26.4: There is cause, there is condition for the purification of beings;
mn60:26.5: beings are purified through cause and condition.
mn60:26.6: There is strength, there is energy, there is human endurance, there is human effort; not all beings, all living creatures, all creatures, all lives are powerless, weak, without energy, shaped by destiny, circumstance, and nature, and experiencing *dukkha* and *sukha* in exactly six classes of birth’—
mn60:26.7: it can be expected of them that, having turned away from these three unwholesome dhammas—bad bodily conduct, bad verbal conduct, and bad mental conduct—they will undertake and practice these three wholesome dhammas—good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct.
mn60:26.9: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do see the danger, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome dhammas, and the benefit in renunciation and the side of purification in wholesome dhammas.
mn60:27.1: Though cause truly exists, he holds the view, ‘There is cause’; that is his right view.
mn60:27.2: Though cause truly exists, he intends, ‘There is cause’; that is his right thought.
mn60:27.3: Though cause truly exists, he speaks the words, ‘There is cause’; that is his right speech.
mn60:27.4: Though cause truly exists, he says, ‘There is cause’; and he does not make himself opposed to those *arahants* who affirm cause.
mn60:27.5: Though cause truly exists, he persuades another, ‘There is cause’; that is persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*].
mn60:27.6: And through that persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*], he neither raises himself up nor puts others down.
mn60:27.7: Thus his bad conduct has already been abandoned, and good conduct is present—
mn60:27.8: and there are also this right view, right thought, right speech, non-opposition to the noble ones, persuasion in true *dhamma* [*saddhammasaññatti*], no self-exaltation, and no disparagement of others. In this way these many wholesome dhammas come to be for him because of right view.
mn60:28.1: In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus: ‘If there is cause, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will be reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn60:28.2: And even setting aside the question of cause, and granting that what those ascetics and brahmins say is true,
mn60:28.3: still this person is praised by the wise here and now: “This person is virtuous, of right view, and one who affirms cause.”
mn60:28.4: If there really is cause, then this person wins both ways—
mn60:28.5: both because he is praised by the wise here and now, and because on the breakup of the body, after death, he will be reborn in a good destination, in a heavenly world.
mn60:28.6: So this sure principle, well grasped and adopted by him, covers both sides and leaves out the unwholesome alternative.
mn60:29.1: There are, householders, some ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:29.2: ‘There are absolutely no beings who are wholly formless.’
mn60:3.1: Then the brahmin householders of Sālā went to the *Bhagavā*. Having approached, some paid homage to the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Some exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā* and, after concluding cordial and memorable conversation, sat down to one side. Some raised their joined palms toward the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Some announced their name and clan in the presence of the *Bhagavā* and sat down to one side. Some sat down to one side in silence. When they were seated to one side, the *Bhagavā* said this to the brahmin householders of Sālā:
mn60:30.1: And among those very ascetics and brahmins, householders, there are some ascetics and brahmins who are direct opponents.
mn60:30.3: ‘There are beings who are wholly formless.’
mn60:30.5: are not those ascetics and brahmins direct opponents of one another?”
mn60:31.1: “In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus—
mn60:31.10: my rebirth there among those devas with form, mind-made, would be assured.
mn60:31.11: But those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:31.12: “There are beings who are wholly formless” — if what those ascetics and brahmins say is true, then this possibility exists:
mn60:31.13: my rebirth there among those formless devas made of saññā would be assured.
mn60:31.14: Now because of form, the taking up of clubs and weapons, quarrels, disputes, arguments, accusations, slander, and false speech are seen.
mn60:31.15: But this is not found at all in what is wholly formless.’
mn60:31.16: Reflecting thus, he practices for disenchantment with form itself, for dispassion toward it, and for its ending.
mn60:31.2: ‘Those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:31.3: “There are absolutely no beings who are wholly formless” — this has not been seen by me.
mn60:31.4: And those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:31.5: “There are beings who are wholly formless” — this is not known by me.
mn60:31.6: And if, not knowing and not seeing, I were to take a one-sided stand and declare—
mn60:31.7: “Only this is true; anything else is empty” — that would not be fitting for me.
mn60:31.8: Those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:31.9: “There are absolutely no beings who are wholly formless” — if what those ascetics and brahmins say is true, then this possibility exists:
mn60:32.1: There are, householders, some ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:33.1: And among those very ascetics and brahmins, householders, there are some ascetics and brahmins who are direct opponents.
mn60:33.5: are not those ascetics and brahmins direct opponents of one another?”
mn60:34.1: “In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus—
mn60:34.10: my rebirth there among those formless devas made of saññā would be assured.
mn60:34.11: But those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:34.12: “There is an absolute ending of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*]” — if what those ascetics and brahmins say is true, then this possibility exists:
mn60:34.13: that I will attain final *nibbāna* in this very life.
mn60:34.14: Those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:34.15: “There is absolutely no ending of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*]” — their view is close to passion, close to bondage, close to delighting, close to fixation, close to clinging/feeding [*upādānāya*].
mn60:34.16: But those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:34.17: “There is an absolute ending of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*]” — their view is close to dispassion, close to non-bondage, close to not delighting, close to non-fixation, close to non-clinging/non-feeding [*anupādānāya*].
mn60:34.18: Reflecting thus, he practices for disenchantment with *bhavas* themselves, for dispassion toward them, and for their ending.
mn60:34.2: ‘Those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:34.3: “There is absolutely no ending of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*]” — this has not been seen by me.
mn60:34.4: And those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:34.5: “There is an absolute ending of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*]” — this is not known by me.
mn60:34.6: And if, not knowing and not seeing, I were to take a one-sided stand and declare—
mn60:34.7: “Only this is true; anything else is empty” — that would not be fitting for me.
mn60:34.8: Those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:34.9: “There is absolutely no ending of *bhava* [*bhavanirodho*]” — if what those ascetics and brahmins say is true, then this possibility exists:
mn60:35.1: These four kinds of persons, householders, are found in the world.
mn60:35.3: Here, householders, one person is a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification.
mn60:35.4: Here, householders, one person is a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others.
mn60:35.5: Here, householders, one person is both a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification, and a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others.
mn60:35.6: Here, householders, one person is neither a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification, nor a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others;
mn60:35.7: not tormenting himself or others, he dwells in this very life free from hunger, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahmā himself.
mn60:36.1: And what, householders, is the person who is a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification?
mn60:36.2: Here, householders, one person is a naked ascetic, one who rejects normal conduct, one who licks his hands …
mn60:36.3: In this way he dwells devoted to the many various pursuits of tormenting and mortifying the body.
mn60:36.4: This is called, householders, the person who is a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification.
mn60:37.1: And what, householders, is the person who is a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others?
mn60:37.2: Here, householders, one person is a butcher of sheep, a butcher of pigs … or else engaged in any other cruel occupation.
mn60:37.3: This is called, householders, the person who is a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others.
mn60:38.1: And what, householders, is the person who is both a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification, and a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others?
mn60:38.2: Here, householders, one person is a king or an anointed noble warrior …
mn60:38.3: They too, threatened with punishment and fear, with tearful faces and weeping, carry out the preparations.
mn60:38.4: This is called, householders, the person who is both a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification, and a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others.
mn60:39.1: And what, householders, is the person who is neither a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification, nor a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others;
mn60:39.2: who, not tormenting himself or others, dwells in this very life free from hunger, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahmā himself?
mn60:4.1: “Householders, is there some teacher agreeable to you, in whom you have gained confidence grounded on reasons?”
mn60:4.2: “There is not, bhante, any teacher agreeable to us in whom we have gained confidence grounded on reasons.”
mn60:4.3: “Since you have not found a teacher agreeable to you, householders, this sure principle should be undertaken and practiced.
mn60:4.4: For when this sure principle is properly undertaken and adopted, it will lead to your welfare and happiness for a long time.
mn60:4.5: And what, householders, is the sure principle?
mn60:40-54.1: Here, householders, a Tathāgata arises in the world, an *arahant*, perfectly awakened …
mn60:40-54.3: having abandoned these five hindrances, the defilements of the mind [*citta*], weakeners of discernment [*paññā*],
mn60:40-54.4: quite secluded [*viveka*] from sensual pleasures, secluded [*viveka*] from unwholesome dhammas, he enters and dwells in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*viveka*].
mn60:40-54.5: With the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], with internal confidence, with a unified [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] mind [*citta*], without thought [*vitakka*], without examination [*vicāra*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*, he enters and dwells in the second *jhāna* …
mn60:40-54.7: he enters and dwells in the fourth *jhāna*.
mn60:5.1: There are, householders, some ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:5.2: ‘There is no gift, no sacrifice, no offering; there is no fruit or result of good and bad kammas; there is no this world, no other world; there is no mother, no father; there are no beings who are spontaneously reborn; and there are no ascetics and brahmins in the world who are rightly accomplished and rightly practicing, who having directly known and realized for themselves this world and the other world make them known.’
mn60:55.1: When his mind [*citta*] is thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he inclines his mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of recollecting past lives.
mn60:55.10: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of existence.’
mn60:55.2: He recollects his many kinds of past lives, that is: one birth, two births … Thus with aspects and details he recollects his many kinds of past lives.
mn60:55.3: When his mind [*citta*] is thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he inclines his mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the passing away and rebirth of beings.
mn60:55.4: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, he sees beings passing away and being reborn, inferior and superior, beautiful and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination … he understands beings as they fare according to their kamma.
mn60:55.5: When his mind [*citta*] is thus endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhite*], purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, he inclines his mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the destruction of the *āsavas* [*āsavānaṁ*].
mn60:55.6: He understands as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’ …
mn60:55.7: he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the practice leading to the ending of the *āsavas* [*āsavanirodhagāminī*].’
mn60:55.8: For one knowing thus and seeing thus, the sensuality-*āsava* [*kāmāsavā*] is liberated from the mind [*citta*], the *bhava*-*āsava* [*bhavāsavā*] is liberated from the mind [*citta*], the blindness [*avijjā*]-*āsava* [*avijjāsavā*] is liberated from the mind [*citta*].
mn60:55.9: When liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn60:56.1: This is called, householders, the person who is neither a self-tormentor, devoted to pursuing self-mortification, nor a tormentor of others, devoted to pursuing the mortification of others;
mn60:56.2: not tormenting himself or others, he dwells in this very life free from hunger, quenched, cooled, experiencing *sukha*, having become Brahmā himself.”
mn60:57.1: When this was said, the brahmin householders of Sālā said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn60:57.3: Just as if someone were to set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what was hidden, or point out the way to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the dark so that those with eyes could see forms, so too Master Gotama has made the *dhamma* clear in many ways.
mn60:57.4: We go for refuge to Master Gotama, to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn60:57.5: May Master Gotama remember us as lay followers who from today have gone for refuge for life.”
mn60:57.6: The Sure Principle is finished, the tenth.
mn60:57.7: The Householder Chapter, the first, is finished.
mn60:58.4: Many Kinds of Feeling, and the Sure Principle as the tenth.
mn60:6.1: And among those very ascetics and brahmins, householders, there are some ascetics and brahmins who are direct opponents.
mn60:6.3: ‘There is gift, there is sacrifice, there is offering; there is fruit and result of good and bad kammas; there is this world, there is the other world; there is mother, there is father; there are beings who are spontaneously reborn; and there are ascetics and brahmins in the world who are rightly accomplished and rightly practicing, who having directly known and realized for themselves this world and the other world make them known.’
mn60:6.5: ‘Are not those ascetics and brahmins direct opponents of one another?’”
mn60:7.1: “Now in this case, householders, those ascetics and brahmins who hold such a doctrine and view as this:
mn60:7.2: ‘There is no gift, no sacrifice … who having directly known and realized for themselves this world and the other world make them known’—
mn60:7.3: it can be expected of them that, having turned away from these three wholesome dhammas—good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, and good mental conduct—they will undertake and practice these three unwholesome dhammas—bad bodily conduct, bad verbal conduct, and bad mental conduct.
mn60:7.5: Because those good ascetics and brahmins do not see the danger, the degradation, and the defilement in unwholesome dhammas, nor the benefit in renunciation and the side of purification in wholesome dhammas.
mn60:8.1: Though the other world truly exists, he holds the view, ‘There is no other world’; that is his wrong view.
mn60:8.2: Though the other world truly exists, he intends, ‘There is no other world’; that is his wrong thought.
mn60:8.3: Though the other world truly exists, he speaks the words, ‘There is no other world’; that is his wrong speech.
mn60:8.4: Though the other world truly exists, he says, ‘There is no other world’; and he makes himself opposed to those *arahants* who know the other world.
mn60:8.5: Though the other world truly exists, he persuades another, ‘There is no other world’; that is persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*].
mn60:8.6: And through that persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*], he raises himself up and puts others down.
mn60:8.7: Thus his good conduct has already been abandoned, and bad conduct is present—
mn60:8.8: and there are also this wrong view, wrong thought, wrong speech, opposition to the noble ones, persuasion in non-true *dhamma* [*asaddhammasaññatti*], self-exaltation, and disparagement of others. In this way these many evil, unwholesome dhammas come to be for him because of wrong view.
mn60:9.1: In this case, householders, a sensible person reflects thus:
mn60:9.2: ‘If there is no other world, then on the breakup of the body this person will have made himself safe.
mn60:9.3: But if there is another world, then on the breakup of the body, after death, this person will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn60:9.4: And even setting aside the question of the other world, and granting that what those ascetics and brahmins say is true,
mn60:9.5: still this person is criticized by the wise here and now: “This person is immoral, of wrong view, and one who holds the doctrine of non-existence.”
mn60:9.6: But if there really is another world, then this person loses both ways—
mn60:9.7: both because he is criticized by the wise here and now, and because on the breakup of the body, after death, he will be reborn in a place of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell.
mn60:9.8: So this sure principle, badly grasped and adopted by him, covers only one side and misses the wholesome alternative.
